claim: Each time a survival-mode dog is approached, called, or chased by a well-meaning person, its fear level toward humans increases — making each failed capture attempt harder to recover from and the dog more likely to run into traffic.
stance_target: Cumulative harm protocol: every failed pursuit attempt compounds the problem — early public education is critical
scope_conditions: Dogs in survival or flight mode
quoted_support: Each time something like this happens it increases the dog's level of fear towards people. When this behavior is being exhibited the dog is said to be in survival or flight mode. The dog will do whatever it needs to do to escape the threat whether that be disappearing into the woods or frantically running into traffic.

claim: Flyers for survival-mode dogs should explicitly instruct the public 'Do not call, approach or chase — he/she is extremely frightened and will run away' and direct sighters to call and report only.
stance_target: Flyer design protocol for flight-mode cases — behavioral guidance to the public is load-bearing content, not optional
scope_conditions: Dogs confirmed or suspected to be in flight/survival mode
quoted_support: Get the message out to the public verbally or on your flyer by describing the dog's behavior in simple terms they can understand, such as, 'Do not call, approach or chase the dog, he/she is extremely frightened and will run away from you.' Direct the person to instead call and report the sighting immediately.
